FT_IC1.evidence: Uses ABCD data; U.S. adolescent sample from neuroimaging study

FT_IC5.evidence: Original fMRI analysis examining neural activation during error processing as a function of cyberbullying victimization

FT_IC2.evidence: Cyberbullying victimization alone fails IC2 per binding clarification. No separate phone/SM variable paired with cyberbullying. While the paper examines neural correlates (error processing, inhibitory control) and mental health outcomes, the exposure is cyberbullying victimization unlinked to a measured SM/phone variable.

notes: Study is longitudinal and uses ABCD fMRI data; examines inhibitory control and error processing neural activation as mediators between cyberbullying and mental health. However, binding rule explicitly excludes cyberbullying alone without measured phone/SM variable.

FT_IC1.evidence: ABCD study data; N=8,979 preadolescents with baseline mean age 9.5 years; U.S. sample

FT_IC5.evidence: Original temporal network analysis using Graphical Vector Autoregressive Model to examine bidirectional associations between screen activities and irritability

FT_IC6.evidence: Novel temporal network approach to screen time and irritability; non-duplicate

FT_IC2.evidence: Step 1: Screen time variables (video streaming, online chatting, TV, social media) are phone/SM-specific and paired with irritability (individual-trait outcome: parent-rated Child Behavior Checklist). Online chatting and social media are explicitly SM platforms; TV and video streaming are screen activities. Other side is irritability (emotion dysregulation), a behavioral/psychological trait.

FT_IC3.evidence: Quantitative results throughout: temporal network coefficients showing bidirectional associations (online chatting predicts decreases in irritability; irritability predicts increased online chatting time). TV and social media unidirectionally predict increased irritability.

FT_IC4.evidence: Inferential temporal network analysis with directed edges; Graphical Vector Autoregressive Model coefficients; results controlled for co-occurring ADHD, anxiety, depressive symptoms

FT_EC7_reason: Cross-sectional carve-out applies. Paper includes cross-sectional Gaussian Graphical Model analysis ('The cross-sectional Gaussian Graphical Model showed that irritability was negatively associated with time spent on video streaming at Year 2'). Per binding rule, if any cross-sectional analysis with SM as DV exists, EC7 does not apply regardless of longitudinal SM-as-DV analyses.
notes: Longitudinal design (baseline, 1-year, 2-year follow-ups) with temporal network analysis. Screen time measured via ABCD Youth Screen Time Survey; irritability via parent-rated CBCL. Both cross-sectional and longitudinal analyses present.

FT_IC1.evidence: ABCD study data; baseline and 2-year follow-up, n=7,043; U.S. adolescent sample

FT_IC5.evidence: Original linear mixed-effect models examining longitudinal associations of lifestyle factors (sleep, screen time, extracurricular activities) with cognitive development

FT_IC6.evidence: Distinct longitudinal cognitive development study; non-duplicate

FT_IC2.evidence: Step 1: Screen time variables (passive watching, social media, social engagement) are phone/SM-specific and paired with cognitive functions (inhibitory control, attention, overall cognitive scores) as individual-trait outcomes. Other side comprises cognitive development measures (inhibitory control, attention, processing speed)—individual-trait neuropsychological outcomes.

FT_IC3.evidence: Quantitative results: longer sleep duration, greater time on nonphysical activities, shorter screen usage associated with greater increases in cognitive scores. Sex moderated associations; specific coefficients reported in mixed-effect models.

FT_IC4.evidence: Linear mixed-effect models with inferential statistics; moderation analysis by sex (negative association between passive watching and inhibitory control/attention stronger in females)

FT_EC7_reason: Screen time is the independent variable (IV); cognitive development is the dependent variable (DV). FT-EC7 only applies when SM/phone/screen time is the DV. Here, the design examines how screen time predicts cognitive development over time.
notes: Longitudinal design with baseline and 2-year follow-up. Screen time broken down by activity type (passive watching, social media, social engagement). Sex moderates several associations. Linear mixed-effect models used for analysis.
